one_line_takeaway: A medical-domain scoping survey that maps hallucination-mitigation techniques (RAG by generation phase, self-refinement, fine-tuning, prompt engineering, unlearning) and argues they need domain adaptation and domain-specific benchmarks like Med-HALT, but contributes no new experiments of its own.
key_finding: The paper argues that general-purpose hallucination-mitigation techniques (RAG, iterative self-refinement/feedback loops, supervised fine-tuning, prompt engineering, unlearning) are promising but under-adapted to medicine, which uniquely demands up-to-date specialized knowledge, strict guideline adherence, and domain-specific evaluation. It highlights that RAG effectiveness hinges on retrieval source quality/authority, that dynamic retrieve-or-not decisions (e.g., ADAPT-LLM) and real-time validation (e.g., EVER) are needed, and that domain-specific benchmarks such as Med-HALT are essential because general evaluations are too coarse. It also surfaces the 'hallucination snowballing' phenomenon (from Zhang et al.) as a mechanism where an initial committal answer drives a supporting-but-wrong explanation.
core_contribution: A scoping/literature study (not an empirical paper) by Pham and Vo (Swinburne University of Technology) that surveys and organizes existing techniques for mitigating hallucination in LLMs on knowledge-based tasks (QA and summarization), with a focus on adapting them to the medical/biomedical domain. Its own contribution is the synthesis: it structures prior work around two research questions (RQ1: effectiveness of current mitigation techniques for general knowledge-based tasks; RQ2: effectiveness for medical QA/summarization), taxonomizes RAG-based methods by generation phase (before/during/after/end-to-end), and compiles insights and future directions (data quality/source authority, dynamic retrieval decisions, real-time validation, conflict resolution, iterative self-refinement, ensemble learning, medical benchmarks). It explicitly builds on and is 'inspired by' Tonmoy et al.'s taxonomy of 32 mitigation techniques rather than proposing a new method.
threat_model: None. This is a reliability/safety-oriented survey about unintentional model hallucination (factual/faithfulness errors), not an adversarial-security paper. There is no attacker, no threat actor, and no defense against a malicious input in the paper. Hallucination is framed as a risk to clinical decision-making and patient safety, not as a security exploit.
limitations: As a scoping study it contributes no new empirical evidence, benchmark, or head-to-head comparison; it does not quantitatively rank the surveyed techniques or run them on a common medical testbed. The medical-domain effectiveness question (RQ2) is answered largely by pointing to a handful of studies rather than by synthesized measurements. Coverage is a v1 arXiv preprint (Aug 2024) with a modest reference list (45 refs). Numbers reported are inherited from cited papers and are not independently reproduced or normalized across methods. No systematic quality assessment, PRISMA-style inclusion/exclusion counts, or risk-of-bias analysis is reported.

datasets_used: No datasets are used by the authors themselves (this is a literature scoping study).

Datasets/benchmarks mentioned as belonging to CITED works: Med-HALT (medical hallucination benchmark, cited from Pal et al.); MedMCQA, PubMedQA, MedQA-USMLE (used by the cited LLM-Synergy work, Yang et al.); Primality Test / Senator Search / Graph Connectivity (three synthetic yes/no datasets from the cited snowballing study, Zhang et al.); PubMed corpus (training data of the cited BioMedLM model).
quantitative_results: All figures below are reported FROM CITED WORKS, not measured by this paper. Snowballing study (Zhang et al. [44]): ChatGPT and GPT-4 gave correct yes/no answers 39.87% and 16.6% of the time respectively, yet detected incorrect claims 67.37% (ChatGPT) and 87.03% (GPT-4) of the time; adding 'Let's think step by step' significantly reduced error rates.

Drug-disease association study (Gao et al. [8], ChatGPT/GPT-3.5): accuracy 74.6-83.5% for true associations and 96.2-97.6% for false associations; adding disease context improved true-association accuracy but slightly reduced false-association accuracy.

Ocular-symptom study (Pushpanathan et al. [28]): evaluated ChatGPT-3.5, ChatGPT-4.0, and Google Bard on 37 common eye/vision questions; ChatGPT-4.0 was most accurate; ChatGPT-4.0 and Bard tended to assert accuracy even when wrong (hallucinating), while ChatGPT-3.5 more often disclaimed.
BioMedLM (Bolton et al. [3]): 2.7-billion-parameter biomedical language model trained on PubMed text (cited as a domain-specific model, no metric quoted by this survey).
The survey itself reports NO original quantitative results (no tables of its own).
baselines_compared: None run by the authors. The paper is a qualitative synthesis; it does not perform a controlled baseline comparison. It organizes cited techniques into categories (RAG before/during/after/end-to-end generation; prompt engineering; supervised fine-tuning; self-refinement/iterative feedback; unlearning; ensemble learning) but provides no common-benchmark leaderboard or numeric ranking among them.
